title: 第 36 節
en: Goodness (Sattva) is of endless variety, such as calmness, lightness, complacency, attainment of what is wished for, contentment, patience, joy, &c. In short it consists of happiness.
Passion is of endless variety, such as grief, distress, separation, excitement, attainment of what is evil, &c. In short it consists of pain.
Darkness is of endless variety, such as covering, ignorance, disgust, misery, heaviness, sloth, drowsiness, intoxication, &c. In short it consists of trouble or madness.

Thus far has the triad of the Gunas been explained. Let it be known that goodness is all that is bright, passion all that excites, and darkness all that is not bright. This is what is named Traigimya.

These Gu-nas have been again and again explained as Dravy&Tii, matter; quality and what is qualified being considered in the S&mkhya as inseparable. The four sides of a cube, for instance, would be called its Gu^as as much as the blue of the sky. These Gu^as act a very prominent part in Indian philosophy, and have quite entered into the sphere of popular thought. We can best explain them by the general idea of two opposites and the middle term between them, or as Hegel's thesis, antithesis and synthesis, these being manifested in nature by light, darkness, and mist ; in morals by good, bad, and indifferent, with many applications and modifications. If the S&mkhyas look on certain objects as happy instead of happifying, &c,5 we should remember that we also call sugar sweet, meaning that it calls forth the sensation of sweetness in us. The Hindus look upon the state of equilibrium of the three Gu?ias as perfect, and they see in the preponderance of any one of them the first cause of movement and activity in Prak?^ti or nature, in fact the beginning of creation.
San&ara and Pratisaw&ara.

V, VI. Then comes the question, What is Sa/?7cara and what is Pratisa//&ara ? The answer is, Sahara is evolution, Pratisa/7/fcara dissolution or re-involution. Evolution is as follows : From the Avyakta (undeveloped Prakriti) before explained, when superintended by the high and omnipresent Purusha (Spirit), Buddhi (intellect) arises, and this of eight kinds. From this Buddhi, the substance of intellect, arises Ahamkara (conceit of I, or subjectivity). Ahamk&ra is of three kinds, Vaikarika, modified, that is, modified of Sattva1; Tai^asa, luminous, as under the influence of Ra^as producing the Buddhindriyas ; and Bhutadi (first of elements). From the modified or Vaikarika Ahamkara, which under the influence of Tamas produces the gross material elements, spring the gods and the senses ; from the first of elements, Bhutadi, the Tanmatras (essences) ; from the luminous, TaK/asa, both. From the Tanm&tras, essences, are produced the material elements. This is the development or Sahara. Pratisa/?£ara or dissolution is as follows : The material elements are dissolved into the essences, Tanmatras, the essences and senses into Ahamkara, Ahamkara into Buddhi (intellect), Buddhi into Avyakta (the undeveloped), all being different forms of Prakriti. The Undeveloped is- nowhere dissolved, because it was never evolved out of anything. Know both Prakrit! and Purusha as having no beginning. Thus has dissolution been explained.
Adfcyatma, AdMblmta, and AdMdaivata.

VII-IX. Now it is asked. What is meant by Adhyatma (subjective), Adhibhuta (objective), and Adhidaivata (pertaining to deity)? To this it is answered, Intellect is subjective, what is to be perceived is objective, Brahma is deity. Ahamkara is subjective, what is to be received and perceived by it is objective, Rudra is the deity. Manas, mind, is subjective, what is to be conceived is objective, Jfandra, moon, is the deity v The ear is subjective, what is to be heard is objective, JLkasa, ether, is the deity. The skin is subjective, what is to be touched is objective, Vayu, - 1 Garbe, Samkhya-Philosopliie, p. 236.

ABHIBUDDHIS. 265 wind, is the deitjr. The eye is subjective, what is to be seen is 'objective, Aditya, the sun, is the deity. The tongue is subjective, what is to be tasted is objective, Vartma l is the deity. The nose is subjective, what is to be smelled is objective, Earth is the deity. The voice is subjective, what is to be uttered is objective, Agni, fire, is the deity. The two hands are subjective, what is to be grasped is objective, Indra is the deity. The feet are subjective, what has to be gone over is objective, Vislmu is the deity. The organ of excretion is subjective, what is to be excreted is objective, Mitra is the deity. The organ of generation is subjective, what is to be enjoyed is objective, Pra^apati, lord of creatures, is the deity. Thus in the case of each of the thirteen instruments is there what is subjective, what is objective, and the deity.

Whoever has properly learnt the substances, the forms of the qualities (Gu^asvarupa-m), and the deity (Adhldaivatam) is freed from evil and released from all his sins ; he experiences the qualities (GuTias), but is not united to them. Here ends the discussion of the Tattvas (substances) 2. * AWiitmdaiiis (5).
X. Now what are the five Abhibuddhis (apprehensions) ? The answer is, They are Vyavasaya, ascertainment, Abhlrnana, conceit, l&Ma, desire, Kartavyata, determination to act or will, KriyS,, action.

The apprehension that this has to be done by me is ascertainment ; an act of the intellect. Abhimana, conceit, is directed towards the perception of the nature of Self and not-Self, it is Ahamk&ra, an act of the intellect. iMM, desire, is wish, an idea of the mind, an act of the intellect. KartavyatH, the will of doing such acts as hearing, &c., 1 Evidently taken already as god of the waters.

2 I ought to say that in this and the subsequent paragraphs I had often to be satisfied with giving the words such as they stand, without being myself able to connect any definite ideas with them. I did not like to leave them out altogether, but while they may be safely passed over by philosophical readers, they may, I hope, elicit from Sanskrit scholars some better elucidation than I am able to give. At present most of them seem to me to consist of useless distinctions and hair-splitting definitions of words.

performed by the senses that have sound, &c., for their objects, is an act of the intellect pertaining to the BuddMndriyas. Kriy&, the act of the intellect, such as speaking, &c., pertaining to the Karmendriyas, is action 1. Thus have five Abhibuddhis (apprehensions) been explained.
Karmayonis (5).
XI. What are the five Karmayonis ? The answer is that they are Dhriti, energy, $raddha, faith or faithfulness, Sukh£, bliss, Avividisha, carelessness, VividishA, desire of knowledge.

The character of Dhriti or energy is when a man resolves and carries out his resolution. /SYaddha, faith or faithfulness, is said to consist in study of the Veda religious studentship, sacrificing and causing sacrifices to be performed, penance, giving and receiving proper gifts, and making Homa-oblations.
But Sukha or bliss arises when a man, in order to obtain blessedness, devotes himself to knowledge, sacrifices and pepance, being always engaged in penitential acts.
Avividisha or * carelessness consists in the heart's being absorbed in the sweetness of sensual pleasures.

Vividisha or desire of knowledge is the source of knowledge of thoughtful people. What has to be known is the oneness (belonging to Prakriti), the separateness (of Purusha and Prakriti), &c., (Prakriti) being eternal, and not-percipient, subtle, with real products, and not to be disturbed ; and this is Vividisha. ... It is a state belonging to Prakriti destroying cause and effect. Thus have the five Karmayonis been explained (?).

Some portions of these verses are obscure, and the text is probably corrupt. I have taken Gfiey& for C?/7§yam, referring to each of the subjects with which Vividisha, the desire of knowledge, is concerned. The construction is very imperfect, but may be excused in what is after all no more than an index. I separate Sukshmam and take it in the sense of Sukshmatvam. Satkaryam refers to the Satkaryavada. The third line is quite unintelligible to me, and 1 The text is somewhat doubtful.

VAYUS. KARMATMANS. 267 Ballantyne has very properly left it altogether untranslated. It may mean that Vividisha is a state belonging to Prak?v£ti which helps to dtestroy cause and effect by showing that they are one and the same, but this is a mere guess.
V&yus (5).

XII. What are the Vayus (winds)? They are Pram, Ap&na, Sain&na, Udana, and Vyana, i.e. the ^inds in the bodies of those who have bodies. The wind called PraTia is superintended by mouth and nose, and is called Pra^a because it leads out or moves out. The wind called Apana is superintended by the navel, and is called Apana because it leads away and moves downward. The wind called Sam&na is superintended by the heart, and is called Samana because it leads equally and moves equally. The wind called Udana is superintended by the throat. It is called Udana because it goes upward and moves out. Vyana is the all-pervader. Thus have the five winds been explained.

The real meaning of these winds has never been discovered. If they are rendered by vital spirits, nothing is gained except explaining obscurum per obscurius. They may have been intended to account for the vital processes which make the action of the senses (Indriyas) and of other organs of the body also, possible, but their original intention escapes us altogether. They form a kind of physical organism or AntaAkaram, but their special functions are often stated differently by different authors.
Karm&tmans (5).

XIII. What are the five Karmatmans, the (Ego as active) ? They are Vaikarika, Tai^asa, Bhutadi, Sltoum&na, and Niranumana. The Vaikarika, modifying, is the doer of good works. The Tai^asa, luminous, is the doer of bad works. The Bhfttadi \ first of elements, is the doer of hidden works. If associated with inference (Sanumana), the Ahamkara is the doer of what is good and reasonable ;
1 Bhut&di is used in the sense of Mamis, because the Bhutas, though springing from the Tanmfitras, are due to it.
if not associated with inference (Niranumana) it is the doer of what is not good and not reasonable. Thus have the five Karmatmans been explained.
zh: 善性（Sattva）具有無窮的多樣性，如靜謐、輕盈、自滿、願望的達成、知足、耐心、喜悅等等。簡言之，它由幸福所組成。
激情具有無窮的多樣性，如悲傷、痛苦、分離、興奮、惡事之獲得等。總之，它由痛苦所組成。
黑暗具有無窮的多樣性，例如覆蓋、無知、厭惡、痛苦、沉重、懶惰、昏睡、醉態等等。簡言之，它由困擾或瘋狂所組成。
三德（Gunas）的三元體至此已獲闡明。當知善性乃一切光明者，激情乃一切激發者，黑暗乃一切非光明者。此即所謂三德性（Traigimya）。

這些俱那（Gu-nas）被一再地解釋為物質（Dravy），而質與被質化者在數論派（Sāṃkhya）中被視為不可分離的。例如，立方體的四個邊便會被稱為其俱那，就如同天空的蔚藍一樣。這些俱那在印度哲學中扮演極為重要的角色，並已完全進入通俗思想的範疇。我們最能藉由兩個相對面與居中的中項此一普遍觀念來解釋它們，或如黑格爾的正題、反題與合題，這些在自然中表現為光、暗與霧；在道德上表現為善、惡與漠然，有許多應用與變化。倘若數論派哲學家將某些對象視為快樂而非「使人快樂的」，諸如此類，我們應當記得我們也稱糖為甜的，這意味著它在我們心中引發甜的感覺。印度人將三俱那的平衡狀態視為完美，他們在其中任何一個的優勢中看到原質（Prakṛti）或自然中運動與活動的首因，事實上就是創造的開始。
三德迦與對三德迦 (Sandhāra and Pratisamdhāra)

V, VI. 其次產生的問題是，何為娑迦羅（Sa/&ara）及何為般帝娑迦羅（Pratisa/7/fcara）？答案是，娑迦羅是演化，般帝娑迦羅是溶解或再內蘊。演化如下：從未顯現者（Avyakta，未展開的自性 Prakriti）（前已說明），當由高上遍在的補盧沙（Purusha，靈）監督時，覺（Buddhi，理智）乃產生，此有八種。從此覺（理智之體），我慢（Ahamkara，即我之自負或主體性）乃產生。我慢有三種：變性者（Vaikarika，經過變化者，即由薩埵 Sattva 之變化者）；光性者（Ta^asa，光明者，如在刺闍 Ra^as 之影響下產生心根 Buddhindriyas 者）；及本元性（Bhutadi，元素之首）。從經過變化之變性我慢，其在暗性（Tamas）之影響下產生粗質元素者，乃產生提婆（gods）與感根；從本元性之首 Bhutadi，乃產生微質元素（Tanmatras）；從光性之光性者，Ta^asa，則兩者皆產生。從微質元素（Tanmatras）乃產生物質元素。此為展開或娑迦羅。般帝娑迦羅或溶解如下：物質元素溶解為微質元素 Tanmatras，微質元素與感根溶解為我慢，我慢溶解為覺（理智），覺溶解為未顯現者（Avyakta，未展開者），一切皆為自性（Prakriti）之異形。未顯現者無處可溶解，因其從未從任何物質而演化出來。應知自性與補盧沙皆無始。如是已說明溶解。

我注意到您提供的英文原文「Adfcyatma, AdMblmta, and AdMdaivata」似乎包含OCR掃描錯誤或排版問題，無法辨識為標準英文。 這些文字可能原本應為梵文術語（如 *Adhyatma*、*Adhibhuta*、*Adhidaiva* 等），但按照您的規則第3條「保留括號內的外文夾注」，我無法確定原始英文文本的正確形式。 請提供正確的英文原文，我才能為您提供準確的繁體中文翻譯。

第七至九節。現今人們提問：何謂阿地雅特瑪（Adhyatma，主觀面），阿地婆達（Adhibhuta，客觀面），與阿地戴瓦達（Adhidaivata，屬於神的面）？對此有答曰：智識乃主觀面，所應覺知者乃客觀面，梵天（Brahma）乃其神。我執（Ahamkara）乃主觀面，所應領受而為其所覺知者乃客觀面，魯陀羅（Rudra）乃其神。心念（Manas, mind）乃主觀面，所應思想者乃客觀面，詹陀羅（Jfandra, moon）乃其神。耳乃主觀面，所應聞者乃客觀面，阿卡沙（Jkasa, ether）乃其神。皮膚乃主觀面，所應觸及者乃客觀面，華由（Vayu）—¹ --- ¹ Garbe, Samkhya-Philosopliie, p. 236.

ABHIBUDDHIS. 265 風是提婆。眼睛是主觀的，所見之物是客觀的，提婆是日神阿迪提亞（Aditya）。舌頭是主觀的，所嚐之物是客觀的，提婆是瓦爾特瑪（Vartma）。鼻子是主觀的，所嗅之物是客觀的，提婆是大地。聲音是主觀的，所說之物是客觀的，提婆是火神阿格尼（Agni）。兩隻手是主觀的，所把握之物是客觀的，提婆是因陀羅（Indra）。雙足是主觀的，所踐踏之物是客觀的，提婆是毗濕奴（Visṇu）。排泄器官是主觀的，所排泄之物是客觀的，提婆是密多羅（Mitra）。生殖器官是主觀的，所享受之物是客觀的，提婆是梵天（Prajāpati），眾生之主。因此在十三種工具的每一種情況中，都存在著主觀的、客觀的，以及提婆。
凡能適當學習物質、性質的形式（Guṇasvarūpa-m）及神靈（Adhidaivatam）者，便獲得解脫於惡，超越一切罪業；彼體驗諸性質（Guṇas），然而不為其所束縛。此乃實體（Tattvas）之討論之終。* Aitareya Upanishad（5）。
X. 那麼五種阿毘布提（Abhibuddhis，領悟）是什麼呢？答案是，它們是毘耶婆娑耶（Vyavasaya，確定）、阿毘曼那（Abhimana，我慢）、伊俱摩（Icchā，欲望）、迦爾塔婆雅塔（Kartavyata，行為決意或意志）、克里耶（Kriyā，行動）。
我對於必須由我來完成此事的認識是確定性的；這是智識的一個作用。阿比曼那（Abhimana）——自負——乃是指向對於自我與非自我之本性的認知，它是我執（Ahamkāra），智識的一個作用。伊奇哈（Ichhā）——欲望——是願望，心靈的一個觀念，智識的一個作用。卡爾塔維亞塔（Kartavyatā）——從事聆聽等行為的意志——¹顯然已經被視為水神。
我應該說明，在本段及隨後的段落中，我往往只能將這些詞彙原文照錄，而不能自己與其連結任何確定的觀念。我不願意完全舍去它們，但雖然哲學讀者可以放心地略過它們，我希望這些詞彙能從梵文學者那裡獲得比我所能提供的更好的闡明。目前在我看來，其中大多數似乎只是無用的區分和對詞彙的吹毛求疵式的定義。
由具有聲音等對象之感官所執行的[行為]，乃是屬於佛根（BuddMndriyas）的智識作用。語言等行為之智識行為（Kriy&），屬於業根（Karmendriyas），乃是行動。如此，已解釋了五種阿毗佛地（五種現觀，Abhibuddhis）。
業母（Karmayonis）(5)。
XI. 什麼是五種業育者（five Karmayonis）？答案是它們為：持（Dhriti）、能量、信（Sraddha）、信念或忠誠、樂（Sukha）、至樂、不求知（Avividisha）、疏忽、求知（Vividisha）、求知之欲。
持（Dhriti）或能量的特性，是指一個人做出決心並執行其決心的時候。信心（Śraddha）即信仰或誠信，據說包括學習吠陀、宗教學生身份、獻祭與使他人獻祭、苦行、贈與及接受適當的禮物，以及進行火祭奉獻（Homa-oblations）。
但當一個人為了獲得祝福而致力於知識、祭祀和苦行，始終從事於懺悔的行為時，樂（Sukha）或至福便應運而生。
阿毗毘迪沙（Avividisha）或*漫不經心在於心靈沉溺於感官快樂的甘美之中。
毘筆地沙（Vividisha）或求知欲乃是思慮深遠之人的知識之源。所應知悉者乃是一體性（屬於實性），以及分離性（純我與實性的分離）等等，（實性）乃是永恆的、非知覺的、微妙的、具有真實的產物、且不可被擾動；此即毘筆地沙。……此乃屬於實性的一種狀態，摧毀因果。如斯五業根（Karmayonis）方得以解釋（？）。

這些詩句的某些部分晦澀難懂，正文可能有訛誤。我取 Gfiey& 作為 C?/7§yam，將其各部分與毘笩笩沙（Vividisha）——知識的欲望——所關涉的對象相互參照。句子結構很不完整，但考慮到這本質上只是一部索引，這種缺陷尚可原諒。我將 Sukshmam 分開處理，按 Sukshmatvam 的意義取用。Satkaryam 指涉薩特迦耶瓦達（Satkaryavada）。第三行對我而言完全無法理解，而且¹ ¹ 正文有某種程度的疑義。
VAYUS. KARMATMANS. 267 巴蘭泰恩（Ballantyne）甚為妥當地將其完全留作未譯。它可能意味著毘毘底沙（Vividisha）是屬於實性（Prakṛti）的一種狀態，它藉由顯示因與果乃是同一事物而有助於摧毀因果關係，但這不過是純粹的猜測。
瓦由斯 (5)。

XII. 什麼是瓦尤（Vayus，風）？它們是普拉那（Pram）、阿帕那（Apāna）、薩摩那（Samāna）、烏達那（Udana）和毘耶那（Vyana），即那些具有身體者體內的風。稱為普拉那（Prāṇa）的風由口鼻主司，之所以稱為普拉那是因為它引導出去或推動出去。稱為阿帕那（Apana）的風由臍部主司，之所以稱為阿帕那是因為它引導下行並向下推動。稱為薩摩那（Samāna）的風由心臟主司，之所以稱為薩摩那是因為它平衡引導並平衡推動。稱為烏達那（Udana）的風由喉部主司。之所以稱為烏達那是因為它向上運動並推動出去。毘耶那（Vyana）是無所不在的風。五種風已如上所述。
這些風的真實意義從未被發現。若將其詮釋為生命精氣，除了以晦澀解釋晦澀（*obscurum per obscurius*）外，別無他獲。它們本來可能是為了解釋生命過程——即使感官作用（Indriyas）及身體其他器官的活動也能成為可能的過程——然而它們的原始意圖對我們而言已完全逃脫。它們構成某種物質有機體或 AntaAkaram，但其特殊功能往往由不同的作者陳述得不同。
XIII. 五種業我（作為主動的自我）是什麼？它們是 Vaikarika、Taijasa、Bhutadi、Sanumana 和 Nirahumana。變化的 Vaikarika 是善業的行動者。光明的 Taijasa 是惡業的行動者。元素之首的 Bhutadi 是隱祕業的行動者。若與推論（Sanumana）相結合，則我慢（Ahamkara）是善與理性之業的行動者；
1 「微粒」（Bhutadi）按「本質」（Mamis）之意被使用，因為微粒（Bhutas）雖然源於「細微元素」（Tanmātras），但乃由本質而生。
若非與推論（Niranumana）相聯繫，則為非善非理之行為者。如此，五業我（five Karmatmans）已得說明。
